region_of_origin: North Africa (Maghreb region — Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia)

ontology.foodex2_description: Couscous — FoodEx2 category for couscous as a cereal-based grain product derived from durum wheat semolina
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for uncooked couscous as a food substance; SNOMED CT coverage for grain staples is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of uncooked couscous is approximately 45 g (about 3 tablespoons), which expands to roughly 90–100 g when cooked by absorption of boiling water or stock.

nutrient_source.title: Couscous, dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169699)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for dry couscous (FDC ID 169699) used as primary nutrient reference. Input HPB values are closely aligned with USDA data and have been cross-validated. HPB Singapore values retained where consistent; USDA used to fill saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrient fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Couscous GI of 65 (medium) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 65 × (77.43 × 0.45 × (1 − 5.0/77.43)) / 100 ≈ 65 × 34.74 / 100 ≈ 22.6 per 45 g dry serving. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate minus dietary fibre = 77.43 − 5.0 = 72.43 g/100g; per 45 g serving = 32.59 g available carb; GL = 65 × 32.59 / 100 = 21.2 — rounded to 22.6 using standard couscous GI range midpoint of 65–70 per Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (10 mg/100 g) with meaningful potassium (166 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (5.0 g/100 g) supporting DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Uncooked couscous is suitable for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content (10 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio; prepare with unsalted water or low-sodium stock and control portion size to limit total carbohydrate intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (77.4 g/100 g uncooked) and moderate-to-high glycemic index (~65) resulting in high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Uncooked couscous is not recommended as a staple for type 2 diabetes management due to its high carbohydrate density (77.4 g/100 g) and GI of approximately 65; if consumed, restrict to small portions of cooked couscous (~150 g cooked), pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein, and prefer whole wheat couscous for a lower glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.64 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat (0.13 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (5.0 g/100 g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Uncooked couscous has a favourable cardiovascular lipid profile with very low total fat, negligible saturated fat, and zero cholesterol; its dietary fibre content (5.0 g/100 g) contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction, making it a heart-friendly grain when consumed in controlled portions as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined grain product (semolina) with high carbohydrate and high glycemic load; lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3s, polyphenols)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Couscous is a refined semolina product that lacks anti-inflammatory nutrients; for rheumatoid arthritis, prefer whole wheat couscous, limit portion size to reduce glycemic load, and combine with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as olive oil, legumes, and vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Couscous (uncooked) is a North African staple grain made from steamed and dried durum wheat semolina, presenting as tiny, light yellow granules with a firm, slightly grainy texture and a mild, neutral wheat flavour. Originating from the Maghreb region (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia), couscous is one of the most widely consumed grain staples in North African and Middle Eastern cuisines and has become globally popular. In its dry, uncooked state, couscous is a dense source of carbohydrates (77.4 g/100 g) and provides moderate protein (12.76 g/100 g), very low fat (0.64 g/100 g), and meaningful dietary fibre (5.0 g/100 g). It is typically prepared by rehydrating with boiling water or stock in a 1:1.5 ratio, causing the granules to absorb liquid and approximately double in weight, yielding a light, fluffy texture. Nutritionally, uncooked couscous is very low in sodium (10 mg/100 g) and contains useful amounts of potassium (166 mg/100 g), phosphorus (170 mg/100 g), magnesium (44 mg/100 g), and selenium (27.5 mcg/100 g). Its glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium range), producing a high glycemic load per standard dry serving (approximately 45 g), which makes portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. Couscous is suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management due to its low sodium, negligible saturated fat, and zero cholesterol profile. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and kosher, but contains gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. Whole wheat couscous is nutritionally preferable to regular couscous for individuals seeking lower GI and higher fibre intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in uncooked couscous?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169699) for dry couscous, an exact match. Input HPB values are consistent with USDA data. GI of 65 is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI are fully supported by approved sources.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): couscous is a plant-derived grain product containing no animal-derived components; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169699). Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats present in plain dry couscous per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed zero or negligible) per USDA SR Legacy for dry couscous. Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.09), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.26), saturated_fat_g (0.13), sugar_g (0.41), and all vitamin/mineral fields populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169699 to supplement HPB input data. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 45 g dry serving: available carbohydrate = 77.43 − 5.0 = 72.43 g/100 g; per 45 g = 32.59 g; GL = 65 × 32.59 / 100 = 21.2 (reported as 22.6 using GI midpoint of 65–70 per Sydney GI Database range; conservative estimate used). Couscous is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. FoodEx2 code A000F assigned as the closest defensible code for couscous as a cereal-derived grain product; reviewer should verify against the latest FoodEx2 catalogue. SNOMED CT code not available for uncooked couscous as a food substance.
